elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we use the solution phase space approach based on the covariant phase space formalism to compute the conserved charges of the BTZ black hole, namely mass, angular momentum, and entropy.
elements.sent-2411-002: Furthermore, we discuss the first law of the BTZ black hole and the Smarr relation.
elements.sent-2411-003: For completeness, outer horizon and inner horizon cases have been all included.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, the results of the three-dimensional Kerr-dS spacetime have also been obtained.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results are consistent with previous investigations.
elements.sent-2411-006: Considering the simplicity of the circumstances, we have presented the most detailed possible information, with the aim of facilitating research in related fields.
elements.sent-2411-007: Symmetry is fundamental in modern physics, appearing in various forms, notably as the symmetry of a theory and the symmetry of a specific solution.
elements.sent-2411-008: In gravitational physics, symmetries of solutions, known as isometries, are represented by Killing vectors.
elements.sent-2411-009: A symmetry in a theory, determined by its action and initial/boundary conditions, remains invariant under transformations that map one solution to another.
elements.sent-2411-010: These transformations can be discrete or continuous, with the latter often described by Lie groups.
elements.sent-2411-011: Continuous symmetries are classified as either global or local, with local symmetries allowing transformations based on arbitrary spacetime functions, leading to gauge theories.
elements.sent-2411-012: In such theories, different solutions can be related through local symmetries, and physical theories assume these solutions are equivalent, forming gauge classes.
elements.sent-2411-013: Gauge symmetries, therefore, represent redundant degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-014: Emmy Noether's theorems link symmetries with conservation laws\cite{Noether:1918zz , with the first theorem associating conserved charges to symmetries and the second constraining field equations in gauge theories.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Following the foundational work of Emmy Noether, the relationship between symmetries and conserved charges has become central to theoretical physics.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, in General Relativity (GR), defining these charges in a covariant manner has proven difficult due to the absence of a globally defined time direction.
elements.sent-2411-017: Early approaches to this problem include Komar's covariant formula for conserved charges associated with Killing vectors\cite{Komar:1958wp and the ADM formalism\cite{Arnowitt:1959ah,Arnowitt:1960es,Arnowitt:1962hi , which defines charges for asymptotically flat spacetimes.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, Bondi and colleagues developed methods for charges at null infinity\cite{Bondi:1962px,Sachs:1962zza , and these concepts were extended to asymptotically anti-de Sitter (AdS)\cite{Ashtekar:1984zz,Ashtekar:1999jx,Aros:1999id , de Sitter (dS) spacetime\cite{Balasubramanian:2001nb , and higher-derivative gravity theories\cite{Deser:2002jk,Deser:2002rt .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Despite their utility, these methods have limitations, such as lack of full covariance and dependence on specific actions or asymptotic field behavior (see Sec.
elements.sent-2411-020: A simple, universal formula that works for every asymptotic behavior and every theory seems to not exist in these settings.
elements.sent-2411-021: This highlights the ongoing challenges in defining conserved quantities in GR.
elements.sent-2411-022: Compared with ADM, Bondi and Komar's methods, the covariant phase space formalism (CPSF) provides a systematic way to obtain conserved charges in generic theories with gauge symmetries, in particular generally covariant theories.
elements.sent-2411-023: This method was initiated in \cite{Lee:1990nz,Henneaux:1992ig,Ashtekar:1987hia,Witten:1986qs,Crnkovic:1986ex,Crnkovic:1987tz and studied further in \cite{Barnich:2007bf,Wald:1993nt,Wald:1999wa,Barnich:2001jy,Szabados:2004xxa,Compere:2007az,Compere:2008us,Harlow:2019yfa,Fiorucci:2021pha,Shi:2020csw,Ciambelli:2022vot .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Wald's approach (or called Wald's formalism) is based on action formulation.
elements.sent-2411-025: Besides, there is another way of formulating the CPSF, given by Barnich et al., based on the equation of motion (EOM)\cite{Barnich:2001jy,Barnich:2007bf .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We shall center on Wald's approach.
elements.sent-2411-027: The crucial object of the CPSF lies in the construction of the phase space and its symplectic structure.
elements.sent-2411-028: For the construction of this phase space, the Lagrangian formulation is required rather than the Hamiltonian one; consequently, its name incorporates ``covariant".
elements.sent-2411-029: After the construction of the phase space, one can deduce surface charges from the symplectic potential.
elements.sent-2411-031: In 2016, based on the CPSM, Hajian et al. formulated the solution phase space method (SPSM) for computing the conserved charges\cite{Hajian:2015xlp , such as mass, angular momentum, electric charge, and entropy, which are associated with the exact symmetries of black hole solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Distinct from the CPSM, the SPSM enables the calculation of conserved charges via integration over nearly any smooth codimension-2 surface encircling the singularity of the black hole, not being confined to the horizon bifurcation surface.
elements.sent-2411-033: Here, black hole entropy is regarded as a conserved charge connected to a linear combination of generators for stationarity, axial isometry, and other symmetries, with coefficients determined by the selected horizon.
elements.sent-2411-034: This methodology facilitates the derivation of the first law of black hole thermodynamics for any chosen horizon in a black hole solution, addressing the problems identified in previous investigations by circumventing the constraints of specific horizons or asymptotic conditions.
elements.sent-2411-035: After the work of Hajian et al., there was some work that applied their methods to other situations\cite{Hajian:2016kxx, Ghodrati:2016vvf, Ding:2020bwa .}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, the circumstances they take into account are relatively complicated.
elements.sent-2411-037: If this method is general, then it must also be applicable to simpler situations.
elements.sent-2411-038: With a simpler case, we can show the details of the method more clearly and know better where we are going.
elements.sent-2411-039: Based on this, we consider the simplest possible scenario, i.e. non-charged rotating BTZ black hole\cite{Banados:1992wn,Banados:1992gq,Carlip:1995qv,Carlip:1994hq,Carlip:1995zj .}
elements.sent-2411-040: In other words, this paper has some pedagogical significance.
elements.sent-2411-041: We strive to provide as many details as possible to show readers how to obtain conserved charges through the SPSM step by step and make everything as reasonable as possible.
elements.sent-2411-042: Some points that need to be noted have also been pointed out.
elements.sent-2411-043: It is hoped that this method will be more widely accepted and applied.
elements.sent-2411-044: Of course, the BTZ black hole itself is also very important.
elements.sent-2411-045: The various problems associated with the semi-classical or quantum aspects of black holes (microstates and entropy, evaporation and unitarity, entanglement, smoothness of internal region, holographic aspects, etc.) arise independently of dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-046: If they can be resolved in a technically more manageable framework, such as in low-dimensional gravity, we can learn valuable lessons about the semi-classical or quantum aspects of dimension-independent black holes.
elements.sent-2411-047: If we want to keep the defining properties of a black hole, an event horizon, then we need at least one dimension of space and one dimension of time.
elements.sent-2411-048: Therefore, the minimum possible size of a black hole toy model is two.
elements.sent-2411-049: But the Einstein tensor vanishes kinematically for any two-dimensional metric, and if we want to make the theory have dynamic effects, we must add some other fields, e.g. scalar field (called dilaton), which corresponds to JT gravity or other models\cite{Mertens:2022irh,Grumiller:2002nm,Blommaert:2018iqz,Grumiller:2021cwg,Blommaert:2018oro,Ruzziconi:2020wrb .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Therefore, the minimum possible dimension of a GR-based black hole toy model is three.
elements.sent-2411-051: On the other hand, the most typical solution of a three-dimensional black hole is the BTZ black hole.
elements.sent-2411-052: For the reasons mentioned above, BTZ black holes and their related three-dimensional gravity have been widely concerned by people, even today.
elements.sent-2411-053: For earlier work on BTZ black holes and three-dimensional gravity, see\cite{Jackiw:1984je,Brown:1988am,Witten:1988hc,Banados:1998gg,Carlip:1998uc,Carlip:2004ba,Witten:2007kt,Myung:2006sq ; For recent work on BTZ black holes and three-dimensional gravity, see\cite{Cotler:2018zff,Geiller:2020edh,Donnay:2016iyk,Cotler:2018zff,Geiller:2020edh,Nguyen:2021pdz,Collier:2023fwi,Upadhyay:2019hyw,Yu:2021rfg,Verheijden:2021yrb,Balthazar:2021xeh,Hennigar:2020drx,Ditta:2022fjz,Craps:2020ahu,Narzilloev:2021jtg,Du:2023nkr,Craps:2021bmz,EslamPanah:2022ihg,Bueley:2022ple,Santos:2021orr,Robbins:2021ion,Jeong:2023rck,Ding:2023niy,Pourhassan:2022cvn,Zeng:2019huf,Pourhassan:2023qpv,Matsueda:2014aoa .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Because BTZ black holes are so important, deepening our understanding of them from any aspect would be beneficial for our community, and we hope our work can make some modest contributions to this.
elements.sent-2411-055: After the work of Hawking et al.\cite{Gibbons:1977mu , in these years, dS spacetime has attracted a lot of attention\cite{Chandrasekaran:2022cip,Maldacena:2019cbz,Balasubramanian:2020xqf,Geng:2021wcq,Shaghoulian:2022fop,Ovalle:2021jzf,Emparan:2022ijy .}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The three-dimensional Kerr-dS spacetime (i.e., KdS$_3$) represents an almost simplest case\cite{Park:1998qk,Wang:2006eb .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Naturally, its thermodynamic properties are a matter of great concern.
elements.sent-2411-058: The computation in KdS$_3$ spacetime using the SPSM is very similar to the case of BTZ black hole.
elements.sent-2411-059: Hence, we also give the results of KdS$_3$ spacetime as a direct extension of the case in the BTZ black hole.

elements.sent-2411-061: The SPSM is based on the CPSF, so before we begin to describe the SPSM formally, let us go to the CPSF first.
elements.sent-2411-062: The phase space is a manifold $\mathcal{M}$ equipped with a symplectic 2-form $\bm{\Omega}$.
elements.sent-2411-063: In order to obtain $\bm\Omega$ for a generally invariant theory, we consider an arbitrary action \begin{equation}S[\Phi]=\intL,\end{equation} where $\bm{L}$ is a Lagrangian $d$-form, $\bm{L =*\mathcal{L =\bm{\epsilon}\mathcal{L}$ with Lagrangian density $\mathcal{L}$ and volume form $\bm\epsilon$, and $\Phi$ denotes all the dynamical fields.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Varying the above action, one can obtain \begin{equation}\deltaL[\Phi]=E_\Phi\delta\Phi+\dd\bm\Theta(\delta\Phi,\Phi),\end{equation} where $\bm{E _\Phi\delta\Phi$ is the bulk term, in which $\delta\Phi$ is a generic field variation and serves as the basis for the tangent space of the phase space $\mathcal{M}$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: When $\bm E_\phi=0$, we have \begin{equation}\deltaL[\Phi]\approx\dd\bm\Theta(\delta\Phi,\Phi),\end{equation} where ``$\approx$" denotes on-shell equalities.
elements.sent-2411-067: The Lee–Wald symplectic form is defined by\cite{Lee:1990nz \begin{equation}\Omega(\delta_{1}\Phi,\delta_{2}\Phi,\Phi):=\int_{\Sigma}\omega(\delta_{1}\Phi,\delta_{2}\Phi,\Phi),\end{equation} where $\Sigma$ is a codimension-1 Cauchy surface and one can verify that $\bm\Omega$ is independent of the choice of $\Sigma$; $\delta_1\Phi$ and $\delta_2\Phi$ are two arbitrary field perturbations on the tangent space of $\mathcal{M}$; (pre-)symplectic form \begin{equation}\bm\omega(\delta_1\Phi,\delta_2\Phi,\Phi):=\delta_1\bm\Theta(\delta_2\Phi,\Phi)-\delta_2\bm\Theta(\delta_1\Phi,\Phi),\end{equation} which is a $(d-1;2)$-form.}

elements.sent-2411-069: In this sense, we call $\bm\Omega$ is conserved.
elements.sent-2411-070: Denoting the set of solutions to the EOM by $\mathcal{P}$, then $(\mathcal{P ;\bm\Omega)$ is the well-defined phase space.}

elements.sent-2411-128: Let's talk about some physics of the BTZ black hole, i.e., give some setups for the next discussion.
elements.sent-2411-129: In this paper, we just talk about a non-charged rotating BTZ black hole in Schwarzschild-like coordinates, which is described by the following metric ansatz \begin{equation}\label{metric} \dd s^{2}=-N^{2}\dd t^{2}+N^{-2}\dd r^{2}+r^{2}(N_{\phi}\dd t+\dd\phi)^{2}\end{equation} with lapse and shift functions \begin{equation}N(r)=\sqrt{-m+\frac{r^{2}}{l^{2}}+\frac{j^{2}}{4r^{2}}},\quad N_{\phi}(r)=-\frac{j}{2r^{2}},\end{equation} where $-\infty<t<\infty$, $0<r<\infty$ and $0<\phi<2\pi$; $m$ is the mass parameter and $j$ is the angular momentum parameter, which are solution parameters we need to consider as mentioned above.
elements.sent-2411-130: In the horizon, the lapse function $N(r)$ vanishes, which gives \begin{equation}r_\pm=\sqrt{\frac{l \left(l m\pm\sqrt{l^2 m^2-j^2}\right)}{2}},\end{equation} which are two coordinate singularities, where $r_+$ corresponds to the black-hole outer horizon, while $r_-$ corresponds to the black-hole inner horizon.
elements.sent-2411-131: When $r_-=r_+$, this is the case for an extremal black hole, where $|j|=ml$\footnote{In the extremal BTZ black hole, $r_0=r_+=r_-$, the metric becomes $\dd s^2=-\frac{(r^2-r_0^2)^2 {l^2 r^2}\dd t^2+\frac{l^2 r^2 {(r^2-r_0^2)^2}\dd r^2+r^2(\dd\phi-\frac{r_0^2 {lr^2}\dd t)^2$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: For the extremal black holes, there is no bifurcation surface, so the idea of deriving entropy as a Noether charge does not work.
elements.sent-2411-133: However, from a physical perspective, one can expect entropy to be a continuous function for near-extremal black holes.
elements.sent-2411-134: Therefore, the entropy of extremal black holes can be obtained by a limiting procedure starting from near-extremal black holes, and the NHEG phase space and mechanics may need to be considered\cite{Compere:2015mza,Compere:2015bca,Hajian:2013lna .}
elements.sent-2411-135: In the SPSM, the entropy need not be computed by an integration over the codimension-2 bifurcation surface of the bifurcate Killing horizon, and it could be computed by integrating the corresponding density over any closed, compact spacelike codimension-2 surface.
elements.sent-2411-136: Therefore, the SPSM can still be used in the extremal case, a specific example see \cite{Hajian:2015xlp , where the integrability is also discussed.}
elements.sent-2411-137: More information for the (near-)extremal black hole, see also \cite{Kunduri:2007vf,Compere:2015mza,Balasubramanian:2009bg,Gupta:2008ki,Hajian:2015eha,Seraj:2016cym,Kunduri:2013gce,Johnstone:2013ioa .. .}
elements.sent-2411-138: We can also get \begin{equation}m=\frac{r_+^2+r_-^2}{l^2},\quad j=\frac{2r_+ r_-}{l}.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-007: First law of black hole thermodynamics
elements.sent-2411-185: From the zero law of black hole thermodynamics, i.e. $T_H=\frac{\kappa {2\pi}$, we can obtain the temperature of the black hole \begin{equation}T_{H_{(\pm)}}=\pm\frac{r_+^2-r_-^2}{2\pi l^2 r_\pm}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Combining results in the last section, we can find \begin{equation}\delta M=T_H\delta S+\Omega_H\delta J.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-187: This is nothing but the first law of black hole thermodynamics for our case.
elements.sent-2411-188: We can also obtain the Smarr relation\cite{Smarr:1972kt,Wang:2006eb incidentally\footnote{For asymptotic flat black holes in any dimenstions, the general Smarr relation is $\frac{D-3 {D-2 M=TS+\Omega J$, where $D$ is the dimension of spacetime\cite{Gauntlett:1998fz .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: It is not difficult to discover the Smarr relation we have obtianed cannot fit the general result in the $D=3$ case, which is related to the effect of the cosmological constant, more details see \cite{Wang:2006eb,Caldarelli:1999xj,Sekiwa:2006qj,Zeng:2019huf .}
elements.sent-2411-190: Note that the Smarr type relations are not universal, unlike the first law; their form depends on the theory and on the asymptotic behavior of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-191: As we said in the introduction, the case of the KdS$_3$ spacetime is very similar to the case of BTZ black hole.

elements.sent-2411-211: In this paper, we discussed how to use the solution phase space method to obtain the conserved charges of BTZ black holes.
elements.sent-2411-212: Subsequently, the first law of thermodynamics was derived by leveraging the previously obtained conserved charge, and the Smarr relation was incidentally obtained.
elements.sent-2411-213: Finally, the results of three-dimensional Kerr-dS spactime have also been obtained.
elements.sent-2411-214: As the ending, we give some necessary comments.
elements.sent-2411-215: The solution phase space method for computing charges proves to be highly effective in asymptotic AdS scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-216: Utilizing alternative methods (e.g., see \cite{Hollands:2005wt for a review) to calculate similar charges might result in infinite values that require regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-217: The solution phase space method works for any spacetime that has Killing vectors, regardless of its asymptotic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-218: Such a spacetime does not necessarily need to be a black hole, as shown in our calculation of Kerr-dS spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-219: Another example, in near-horizon geometries, this method functions effectively (e.g., see \cite{Hajian:2015xlp for extremal Kerr-Newman near horizon geometry); however, the ADM formalism or its extensions and the Komar integral method all prove unsuccessful.}
elements.sent-2411-220: In the solution phase space method, one does not need to take the integration surface $\partial\Sigma$ to the bifurcation surface of the horizon or $\bm i^0$ at infinity, unlike in \cite{Iyer:1994ys .}
elements.sent-2411-221: The solution phase space method can also be used for the inner horizon and there exists an inner horizon first law \cite{Castro:2012av as shown in our calculations.}
elements.sent-2411-222: As the most direct generalization, one can consider the charged BTZ black hole\cite{Banados:1992wn , where the gauge symmetry of the electromagnetic field should be considered.}
elements.sent-2411-223: For this case, readers can refer to \cite{Ghodrati:2016vvf .}
elements.sent-2411-224: Besides, one can also consider adding more other fields, e.g., dilaton\cite{Chan:1994qa .}
elements.sent-2411-225: In principle, the new surface charges would be added\cite{Frodden:2019ylc .}
elements.sent-2411-226: One can apply the solution phase space method to these cases.
elements.sent-2411-227: It is well known that three-dimensional gravity has a gauge formulation of the Chern-Simons type \cite{Achucarro:1986uwr, Witten:1988hc .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Thus, one can extend our calculation to this scenario and contemplate how our derivation is associated with this case in this form.
elements.sent-2411-229: In recent years, BTZ-like black holes have attracted people's attention\cite{Ditta:2022fjz,Ding:2023niy , and theoretically, one can also try to extend our method to this situation.}
elements.sent-2411-230: Naturally, other variants of BTZ black holes can also be taken into account\cite{Hennigar:2020drx,Narzilloev:2021jtg,EslamPanah:2022ihg,Pourhassan:2023qpv .}
elements.sent-2411-231: As we mentioned above, the (near-)extremal case has unique features\cite{Compere:2015mza,Compere:2015bca,Hajian:2013lna,Kunduri:2007vf,Compere:2015mza,Balasubramanian:2009bg,Gupta:2008ki,Kunduri:2013gce,Johnstone:2013ioa,Craps:2020ahu , so extending our discussion to this case would be interesting.}

elements.sent-2411-233: Due to the universality of the solution space method, in principle, one is capable of investigating all types of spacetime that satisfy Killing symmetry through it as mentioned above.

elements.sent-2411-263: Here we would like to write down the explicit expression of the Christoffel symbol for the BTZ black hole in Schwarzschild-like coordinates.

elements.sent-2411-267: Following Ref. \cite{Hajian:2015xlp , we know that integrability condition, $(\delta_1\delta_2 -\delta_2\delta_1)Q_\xi(\Phi)=0$, is equivalent to \begin{equation}\oint_{\partial\Sigma}\left(\xi\cdot\omega(\hat{\delta}_{1}\Phi,\hat{\delta}_{2}\Phi,\Phi)+k_{\hat{\delta}_{1}\eta}(\hat{\delta}_{2}\Phi,\Phi)-k_{\hat{\delta}_{2}\eta}(\hat{\delta}_{1}\Phi,\Phi)\right)\approx 0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-268: If $\xi$ is a linear combination of parameter-independent Killing vectors, i.e. exact symmetries, whose charges are integrable, the first term can be removed, and the above relation can be further reduced to \begin{equation}\oint_{\partial\Sigma}\left(k_{\hat{\delta}_{1}\eta}(\hat{\delta}_{2}\Phi,\Phi)-k_{\hat{\delta}_{2}\eta}(\hat{\delta}_{1}\Phi,\Phi)\right)=\delta_2 Q_{\eta_1}-\delta_1 Q_{\eta_2}\approx 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-269: It is easy to check that $\partial_t, \partial_\phi$ are integrable from the above relations.
elements.sent-2411-270: Let's consider their generally linear combination, \begin{equation}\xi=A\partial_t+B\partial_\phi,\end{equation} where $A$ and $B$ are some constants over spacetime but functions of the parameters of BTZ black hole, i.e. $A=A(m,j), B=B(m,j)$.
elements.sent-2411-271: Since the tangent space of $\mathcal{S _p$, $T_{\mathcal{S _p}$, for BTZ black hole is two dimensional and spanned by $\pdv{\Phi {m}\delta m$ and $\pdv{\Phi {j}\delta j$, we can just choose $\hat\delta_1\Phi=\pdv{\Phi {m}\delta m$ and $\hat\delta_2\Phi=\pdv{\Phi {j}\delta j$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-272: Then we have \begin{equation}\hat\delta_2 Q_{\xi_1}-\hat\delta_1 Q_{\xi_2}= \hat\delta_2 Q_{\pdv{A}{m}\delta m\partial_t+\pdv{B}{m}\delta m\partial_\phi}-\hat\delta_1 Q_{\pdv{A}{j}\delta j\partial_t+\pdv{B}{j}\delta j\partial_\phi}\nonumber = \pdv{A}{m}\delta m\hat\delta_2 Q_{\partial_t}+\pdv{B}{m}\delta m\hat\delta_2 Q_{\partial_\phi}+\pdv{A}{j}\delta j\hat\delta_1 Q_{\partial_t}+\pdv{B}{j}\delta j\hat\delta_1 Q_{\partial_\phi}\nonumber = (\pdv{A}{j}-\pdv{B}{m})\delta m\delta j,\end{equation} in the second step, we have used the fact that the charges for the linear combination of exact symmetries are the same linear combination of the corresponding charges.
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}\hat\delta_2 Q_{\xi_1}-\hat\delta_1 Q_{\xi_2}= \hat\delta_2 Q_{\pdv{A}{m}\delta m\partial_t+\pdv{B}{m}\delta m\partial_\phi}-\hat\delta_1 Q_{\pdv{A}{j}\delta j\partial_t+\pdv{B}{j}\delta j\partial_\phi}\nonumber = \pdv{A}{m}\delta m\hat\delta_2 Q_{\partial_t}+\pdv{B}{m}\delta m\hat\delta_2 Q_{\partial_\phi}+\pdv{A}{j}\delta j\hat\delta_1 Q_{\partial_t}+\pdv{B}{j}\delta j\hat\delta_1 Q_{\partial_\phi}\nonumber = (\pdv{A}{j}-\pdv{B}{m})\delta m\delta j,\end{equation}
